}} [[File:Birds of Sweden 2016 53.jpg|thumb|Adult ''C. m. monedula'', Vaxholm, Sweden]] The '''western jackdaw''' ('''''Coloeus monedula'''''), also known as the '''Eurasian jackdaw''', the '''European jackdaw''', or simply the '''jackdaw''', is a [[passerine]] bird in the [[Corvidae|crow family]]. It is found across Europe, [[Palearctic|western Asia]] and western North Africa; it is mostly [[Bird migration|resident]], although northern and eastern populations [[Bird migration|migrate]] south in the winter. Four [[subspecies]] are recognised, which differ mainly in the darker or paler greyscale of the [[plumage]] on the head and nape. [[Carl Linnaeus|Linnaeus]] first [[Species description|described]] it formally, giving it the name ''Corvus monedula''. The common name derives from the word ''jack'', denoting "small", and '''daw''', a less common [[synonym]] for "jackdaw", and the native English name for the bird. Measuring {{convert|34|–|39|cm|in}} in length, the western jackdaw is a black-plumaged bird with a grey [[nape]] and distinctive pale [[Iris (anatomy)|iris]]. It is [[gregarious]] and vocal, living in small groups with a complex social structure in farmland, open woodland, on coastal cliffs, and in urban settings. Like its relatives, jackdaws are intelligent birds, and have been observed using tools. An [[omnivore|omnivorous]] and [[opportunistic feeder]], it eats a wide variety of plant material and [[invertebrate]]s, as well as [[food waste]] from urban areas. Western jackdaws are [[Monogamous pairing in animals|monogamous]] and build simple nests of sticks in cavities in trees, cliffs, or buildings. About five pale blue or mint green eggs with brown to grey speckles are laid and incubated by the female. The young fledge in four to five weeks. ==Systematics== ===Etymology=== The western jackdaw was one of the many species originally described by [[Carl Linnaeus]] in his 18th century work ''[[Systema Naturae]]''.{{cite book |last=Linnaeus |first=C. |author-link=Carl Linnaeus |title=Systema Naturae per Regna Tria Naturae, Secundum Classes, Ordines, Genera, Species, cum Characteribus, Differentiis, Synonymis, Locis. Tomus I. Editio Decima, Reformata |volume=1 |publisher=Laurentius Salvius |location=[[Stockholm|Holmiae]] |language=la |date=1758 |page=105 |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/727010}} Owing to its supposed fondness for picking up coins, Linnaeus gave it the binomial name ''Corvus monedula'', choosing the [[Specific name (zoology)|specific name]] ''mǒnēdŭla'', which is derived from ''[[moneta]]'', the Latin stem of the word "money".{{cite book | last = Simpson | first = D.P. | title = Cassell's Latin Dictionary | publisher = Cassell| year = 1979 | edition = 5th | location = London, United Kingdom | page = 883 | isbn = 978-0-304-52257-6}}{{cite book |last=Valpy |first=Francis Edward Jackson |title=An Etymological Dictionary of the Latin Language |date=1828 |publisher=A. J. Valpy |location=London, United Kingdom |isbn=978-1-4021-7384-4 |page=[https://archive.org/details/anetymologicald00valpgoog/page/n300 268] |url=https://archive.org/details/anetymologicald00valpgoog}} Jackdaws are now placed in the genus ''[[Coloeus]]'', from the [[Ancient Greek]] κολοιός (''koloios'') for jackdaw,{{cite book |last=Jobling |first=James |title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names |date=2010 |publisher=Helm |location=London |isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 |page=114 |url=http://nature.baikal.ru/files/605/1408125013.pdf }} while most older works retained the two jackdaw species in ''Corvus''.{{cite book |editor1-last=Mayr |editor1-first=Ernst |editor2-last=Greenway |editor2-first=James C. Jr |title=Check-List of Birds of the World |volume=15 |date=1962 |publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology |location=Cambridge, Massachusetts |page=261 |url=https://archive.org/stream/checklistofbirds151962pete#page/261/mode/1up }} The original [[Old English]] words ''ċēo'' and ''ċeahhe'' (pronounced with initial ''ch'') gave modern English "[[chough]]"; [[Geoffrey Chaucer|Chaucer]] sometimes used this word to refer to the western jackdaw, as did [[William Shakespeare|Shakespeare]] in ''[[Hamlet]]'' although there has been debate about which species he was referring to.{{cite book |last1=Shakespeare |first1=William |author-link1=William Shakespeare |last2=Edwards |first2=Philip |title=Hamlet, Prince of Denmark |url=https://archive.org/details/hamletprincedenm00shak |url-access=limited |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=Cambridge, United Kingdom |date=2003 |page=[https://archive.org/details/hamletprincedenm00shak/page/n254 241] |isbn=978-0-521-53252-5}} This [[onomatopoeia|onomatopoeic]] name, based on the western jackdaw's call, now refers to corvids of the genus ''[[Pyrrhocorax]]''; the [[red-billed chough]] (''Pyrrhocorax pyrrhocorax''), formerly particularly common in [[Cornwall]], became known initially as the "Cornish chough" and then just the "chough", the name transferring from one species to the other.{{cite book |last1=Cocker |first1=Mark |last2=Mabey |first2=Richard |title=Birds Britannica |date=2005 |location=London, United Kingdom |publisher=Chatto & Windus |isbn=978-0-7011-6907-7 |pages=406–8}} The common name ''jackdaw'' first appeared in the 16th century, and is thought to be a compound of the forename ''Jack'', used in animal names to signify a small form (e.g. [[jack snipe]]), and the archaic native English word ''daw''.{{cite book |last=Partridge |first=Eric |date=1966 |title=Origins: A Short Etymological Dictionary of Modern English |publisher=Routledge|isbn=0-203-37182-8 }} Formerly, western jackdaws were simply called "daws".[[#Goodwin|Goodwin]], p. 78 The ''jack'' part of the common name is again likely of onomatopoeic origin from the metallic ''chyak'' call,{{cite book |last=Lockwood |first=W. B. |title=The Oxford book of British bird names |publisher=Oxford University Press |publication-place=Oxford; New York |date=1984 |isbn=0-19-214155-4 |page=}}{{cite web |last=Gains |first=David |title=British Garden Birds: Jackdaw |date=1 July 2010 |url=http://www.garden-birds.co.uk/birds/jackdaw.htm |access-date=27 April 2012 |publisher=self}} but this is not supported by the ''[[Oxford English Dictionary]]''.{{cite encyclopaedia |url=http://dictionary.oed.com/cgi/entry/50122724 |encyclopedia=Oxford English Dictionary |title=Jackdaw, ''n.'' |edition= 2nd |date=1989}} ''Daw'', first used for the bird in the 15th century, is held by the ''Oxford English Dictionary'' to be derived from the postulated [[Old English]] ''dawe'', with [[cognate]]s in [[Old High German]] ''tāha'', [[Middle High German]] ''tāhe'' or ''tāchele'', and modern German ''Dahle'' or ''Dohle'', and dialectal ''Tach, Dähi, Däche'' and ''Dacha''; it is likewise of onomatopoeic origin, based on another of the commonest calls the bird makes. Names in English dialects are numerous. Scottish and north English dialects have included ''ka'' or ''kae'' since the 14th century. The [[Midlands (England)|Midlands]] form of this word was ''co'' or ''coo''. ''Caddow'' is potentially a compound of ''ka'' and ''dow'', a variant of ''daw''. Other dialectal or obsolete names include ''caddesse'', ''cawdaw'', ''caddy'', ''chauk'', ''college-bird'', ''jackerdaw'', ''jacko'', ''ka-wattie'', ''chimney-sweep bird'' (from their nesting propensities), and ''sea-crow'' (from the frequency with which they are found on coasts). It was also frequently known quasi-nominally as ''Jack''.{{cite book |last1=Mullarney |first1=Killian |last2=Svensson |first2=Lars |last3=Zetterstrom |first3=Dan |last4=Grant |first4=Peter |title=Collins Bird Guide | year = 1999 |publisher=Collins |isbn=978-0-00-219728-1 |page=335|title-link=Collins Bird Guide }}{{cite book|last=Swan|first=H. Kirke |title=A Dictionary of English and Folk-Names of British Birds, With their History, Meaning and First Usage: and the Folk-lore, Weather-lore, Legends, etc., Relating to the More Familiar Species |location=London, United Kingdom |publisher=Witherby and Co |date=1913 |doi=10.5962/bhl.title.6948 |url=https://archive.org/details/dictionaryofengl00swan}}{{cite book |last=Wright |first=Joseph |title=The English Dialect Dictionary |url=https://archive.org/details/in.ernet.dli.2015.13022 |location=London, United Kingdom |publisher=Henry Frowde |date=1898–1905}}{{cite book |last=Swainson |first=Charles |title=Provincial Names and Folk Lore of British Birds |url=https://archive.org/details/provincialnames00swaigoog |date=1885|location=London, United Kingdom |publisher=Trübner and Co}} An archaic [[collective noun]] for a group of jackdaws is a "clattering".First recorded in [[John Lydgate]]'s ''Debate between the Horse, Goose and Sheep'' (c.1430) as "A clatering of chowhis", and then in [[Juliana Berners]]' ''[[Book of Saint Albans]]'' (c.1480), as "a Clateryng of choughes". Another name for a flock is a "train".{{cite book |last=White |first=Gilbert |author-link=Gilbert White |date=1833 |title=The Natural History of Selborne |location=London, United Kingdom |publisher=N. Hailes |page=163}} ===Taxonomy=== A study in 2000 found that the genetic distance between western jackdaws and the other members of ''Corvus'' was greater than that within the rest of the genus.{{cite journal |last1=Kryukov |first1=A. P. |last2=Suzuki |first2=H. |date=2000 |title=Phylogeography of Carrion, Hooded and Jungle crows (Aves, Corvidae) Inferred From Partial Sequencing of the Mitochondrial Cytochrome ''b'' Gene |journal=Russian Journal of Genetics |volume=36 |pages=922–29}} This led [[Pamela C. Rasmussen|Pamela Rasmussen]] to reinstate the genus name ''Coloeus'', created by [[Johann Jakob Kaup|Johann Kaup]] in 1829,{{cite book|url=https://archive.org/stream/skizzirteentwick01kaup#page/114/mode/2up/|page=114 |title=Skizzirte Entwickelungs-Geschichte und natürliches System der europäischen Thierwelt: Erster Theil welcher die Vogelsäugethiere und Vögel nebst Andeutung der Entstehung der letzteren aus Amphibien enthält| author=Kaup, J. J. | publisher=Carl Wilhelm Leste |place=Darmstadt und Leipzig| year=1829}} in her ''[[Birds of South Asia]]'' (2005),{{cite book |last1=Rasmussen |first1=Pamela C. |author-link1=Pamela C. Rasmussen |last2=Anderton |first2=John C. |author-link2=John C. Anderton |date=2005 |title=Birds of South Asia: The Ripley Guide |volume=2 |isbn=978-84-87334-67-2 |page=598 |publisher=Smithsonian Institution and Lynx Edicions |location=Washington, D.C. and Barcelona, Spain}} a treatment also used in a 1982 systematic list in German by [[Hans Edmund Wolters]].{{cite book |last=Wolters |first=Hans E. |title=Die Vogelarten der Erde |publisher=Paul Parey |location=Hamburg & Berlin, Germany |date=1982 |language=de |isbn=978-3-490-09118-5}} A study of corvid [[phylogeny]] undertaken in 2007 compared DNA sequences in the [[mtDNA control region|mitochondrial control region]] of several corvids. It found that the western jackdaw, and the closely related [[Daurian jackdaw]] (''C. dauuricus'') of eastern [[Russia]] and [[China]], were [[basal (phylogenetics)|basal]] to the core ''Corvus'' [[clade]]. The names ''Coloeus monedula'' and ''Coloeus dauuricus'' have since been adopted by the [[International Ornithological Congress]] in their official list.{{cite web|url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/n-vireos.html |title=Vireos, Crows & Allies |editor=Gill, F. |editor2=Donsker, D. |date=30 March 2011 |work=IOC World Bird List: Version 2.8.3 |publisher=WorldBirdNames.org |access-date=29 June 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110917184945/http://www.worldbirdnames.org/n-vireos.html |archive-date=17 September 2011 }} The two species of jackdaw have been reported to [[Hybrid (biology)|hybridise]] in the [[Altai Mountains]], southern [[Siberia]], and [[Mongolia]]. Analysis of the [[mitochondrial DNA]] of specimens of the two species from their core ranges show them to be genetically distinct.{{Cite journal | last1 = Haring | first1 = Elisabeth | last2 = Gamauf | first2 = Anita | last3 = Kryukov | first3 = Alexey | title = Phylogeographic patterns in widespread corvid birds | journal = Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution | year = 2007 | doi = 10.1016/j.ympev.2007.06.016 | url = http://ibss.febras.ru/files/00006265.pdf | pages = 840–62 | volume = 45 | issue = 3 | pmid = 17920300 | access-date = 2015-09-04 | archive-date = 2020-11-29 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20201129022531/http://www.ibss.febras.ru/files/00006265.pdf }} ===Subspecies=== [[File:Coloeus monedula -Munkedal, Vastra Gotaland, Sweden-8.jpg|thumb|right|''C. m. monedula'' in Sweden. This subspecies has a whitish partial collar.]] There are four recognised subspecies of the western jackdaw.{{ cite web | author=AviList Core Team | date=2025 | title=AviList: The Global Avian Checklist, v2025 | doi=10.2173/avilist.v2025 | doi-access=free | url=http://www.avilist.org/checklist/v2025/ | access-date=2026-03-21 }} All the European subspecies [[intergrade]] where their populations meet.[[#Goodwin|Goodwin]], p. 75 ''C. m. monedula'' intergrades into ''C. m. soemmerringii'' in a transition zone running from [[Finland]] south across the [[Baltic region|Baltic]] and eastern [[Poland]] to [[Romania]] and [[Croatia]].{{cite journal |author=Offereins, Rudy |date=2003 |title=Identification of eastern subspecies of Western Jackdaw and occurrence in the Netherlands |journal=Dutch Birding |volume=25 |issue=4 |pages=209–220 |url=http://calidris.home.xs4all.nl/jackdaw.htm}} *The '''Nordic jackdaw''' (''C. m. monedula'') (Linnaeus, 1758), the [[nominate subspecies]], is found in eastern Europe. Its range extends across [[Scandinavia]], from southern Finland south to [[Esbjerg]] and [[Haderslev]] in [[Denmark]], through eastern [[Germany]] and Poland, and south across eastern central Europe to the [[Carpathian Mountains]] and northwestern Romania, [[Vojvodina]] in northern [[Serbia]], and [[Slovenia]].[[#Cramp|Cramp]], p. 120. It breeds in south-eastern [[Norway]], southern [[Sweden]], and northern and eastern Denmark, with occasional wintering in [[England]] and [[France]]. It has been recorded as a rare [[Vagrancy (biology)|vagrant]] to [[Spain]].{{cite journal |author1=Dies, J. I. |author2=Lorenzo, J. A. |author3=Gutierrez, R. |author4=García, E. |author5=Gorospe, G. |author6=Marti-Aledo, J. |author7=Gutiérrez, P. |author8=Vidal, C. |author9=Sales, S. |author10=López-Velasco, D. |title=Report on Rare Birds in Spain 2007 |date=2009 |journal=Ardeola |volume=56 |issue=2 |pages=309–44}} It has a pale [[nape]] and sides of the neck, a dark throat, and a light grey partial collar of variable extent. *The '''Western Eurasian jackdaw''' (''C. m. spermologus'') ([[Louis Pierre Vieillot|Vieillot]], 1817) occurs in western, central and southern Europe and North Africa, from the [[British Isles]], the [[Netherlands]] and the [[Rhineland]] in the north, through western [[Switzerland]] into [[Italy]] in the southeast, and the [[Iberian Peninsula|Iberian peninsula]] and [[Morocco]] in the south. It winters in the [[Canary Islands]] and [[Corsica]]. The name ''"spermologus"'' comes from the Greek σπερμολόγος, ''a picker of seeds''.{{cite web |title=''spermologos'' |work=Strong's Greek 4691 |url=http://concordances.org/greek/4691.htm |access-date=15 May 2012}} It is darker in colour than the other subspecies and lacks the whitish border at the base of the grey nape. *The '''Eastern Eurasian jackdaw''' (''C. m. soemmerringii'') ([[Gotthelf Fischer von Waldheim|Fischer von Waldheim]], 1811) is found in northeastern Europe and northern and central Asia from the former [[Soviet Union]] to [[Lake Baikal]] and northwestern Mongolia and south to [[Turkey]], [[Israel]] and the eastern [[Himalayas]]. Its southwestern limits are Serbia and southern Romania. It winters in Iran and northern India ([[Kashmir]]). [[Johann Fischer von Waldheim]] described this [[taxon]] as ''Corvus soemmerringii'' in 1811, noting its differences from populations in western Europe.{{cite journal |last=Fischer |first=G. |date=1811 |title=Notice sur la Choucas de la Russie |journal=Mémoires de la Société Impériale des Naturalistes de Moscou |volume=1 |page=3 |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/38568#page/90/mode/1up |access-date=17 May 2012}} Its subspecific name was given in honour of the German anatomist [[Samuel Thomas von Sömmerring]]. It is distinguished by the nape and the sides of the neck being paler, creating a contrasting black crown and lighter grey part collar. *The '''Algerian jackdaw''' (''C. m. cirtensis'') ([[Walter Rothschild, 2nd Baron Rothschild|Rothschild]] and [[Ernst Hartert|Hartert]], 1912) is found in Morocco and [[Algeria]] in Northwest Africa. It was also formerly found in [[Tunisia]]. The name ''"cirtensis"'' refers to the ancient city of [[Cirta]] in [[Numidia]]. The [[plumage]] is duller and more uniformly dark grey than the other subspecies, with the paler nape less distinct. == Description == [[File:Jackdaw - up close and personal (552502080).jpg|right|thumb|Adult ''C. m. spermologus'', showing the [[Beak#Rictal bristles|rictal bristles]] cover much of the bill.]] [[File:Coloeus monedula, juvenile, Newcastle upon Tyne, England.jpg|right|thumb|Juvenile ''C. m. spermologus'', Newcastle upon Tyne, England.]] [[File:CROP. Corvus monedula, naakka, Naantalin hautausmaa, Naantali, 8.11.2011..jpg|thumb|right|Partially [[Leucism|leucistic]] individual with white feathers in Naantali cemetery, [[Naantali]], [[Finland]]]] The western jackdaw measures {{convert|34|–|39|cm|in}} in length and weighs around {{convert|240|g|oz}}.[[#Goodwin|Goodwin]], p. 74[[#Cramp|Cramp]], pp. 138–139. Most of the plumage is a shiny black, with a purple (in subspecies ''monedula'' and ''spermologus'') or blue (in subspecies ''cirtensis'' and ''soemmerringii'') sheen on the crown,[[#Cramp|Cramp]], p. 139. forehead, and [[Flight feather#Secondaries|secondaries]], and a green-blue sheen on the throat, [[Flight feather#Primaries|primaries]], and tail. The cheeks, nape and neck are light grey to greyish-silver, and the underparts are slate-grey. The legs are black, as is the short stout bill, the length of which is about 75% of the length of the rest of the head. There are [[Beak#Rictal bristles|rictal bristles]] covering around 40% of the maxilla and 25% of the lower mandible. The [[iris (anatomy)|irises]] of adults are greyish or silvery white while those of juveniles are light blue, becoming brownish before whitening at around one year of age. The sexes look alike,{{cite book |last1=Porter |first1=R. F. |last2=Christensen |first2=S. |last3=Schiermacher-Hansen |first3=F. |title=Birds of the Middle East |location=Princeton, New Jersey |publisher=Princeton University Press |date=1996 |page=405}} though the head and neck plumage of male birds fades more with age and wear, particularly just before [[Moulting#Birds|moulting]].[[#Cramp|Cramp]], p. 137. Western jackdaws undergo a complete [[Moulting|moult]] from June to September in the western parts of their range, and a month later in the east. The purplish sheen of the cap is most prominent just after moulting. Immature birds have duller and less demarcated plumage. The head is a sooty black, sometimes with a faint greenish sheen and brown feather bases visible; the back and side of the neck are dark grey and the underparts greyish or sooty black. The tail has narrower feathers and a greenish sheen. There is very little geographic variation in size. The main differences are the presence or absence of a partial whitish collar at the base of the nape and variations in the colour of the nape and the tone of the underparts. Central Asian populations have slightly larger wings, while western populations have slightly heavier bills. Body colour becomes darker in mountain regions and humid climates further north, and paler elsewhere. However, individual variation, particularly in juveniles and during the months before moulting, can often exceed geographic differences. The western jackdaw is a skilled flyer that can manoeuvre tightly, tumble and glide. It has distinctive jerky wingbeats when flying, though these are not apparent during [[bird migration|migration]]. [[Wind tunnel]] experiments show that the preferred gliding speed is between {{convert|6|and|11|m}} per second and that the wingspan decreases as the bird flies faster.{{cite journal |last1=Rosen |first1=Mikael |last2=Hedenstrom |first2=Anders |date=2001 |title=Gliding Flight in a Jackdaw: A Wind Tunnel Study |journal=Journal of Experimental Biology |volume=204 |issue=6 |pages=1153–66 |doi=10.1242/jeb.204.6.1153 |pmid=11222131 |url=http://jeb.biologists.org/content/204/6/1153.full.pdf }} On the ground, western jackdaws have an upright posture and strut briskly, their short legs giving them a rapid gait. They feed with their heads held down or horizontally. Within its range, the western jackdaw is unmistakable; its short bill and grey nape are distinguishing features. From a distance, it can be confused with a [[Rook (bird)|rook]] (''Corvus frugilegus''), or when in flight, with a [[Columbidae|pigeon]] or [[chough]].[[#Cramp|Cramp]], p. 121. Flying western jackdaws are distinguishable from other corvids by their smaller size, faster and deeper wingbeats and proportionately narrower and less fingered wing tips. They also have shorter, thicker necks, much shorter bills and frequently fly in tighter flocks. They can be distinguished from choughs by their uniformly grey underwings and their black beaks and legs.{{cite book |editor1-last=Snow |editor1-first=David |editor2-last=Perrins |editor2-first=Christopher M. |title=The Birds of the Western Palearctic: Concise Edition (2 volumes) |date=1998 |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=Oxford, United Kingdom |isbn=978-0-19-854099-1 |pages=1466–68}} The western jackdaw is very similar in morphology, behaviour, and calls to the Daurian jackdaw, with which its range overlaps in western Asia. Adults are readily distinguished, since the Daurian has a pied plumage, but immature birds are much more similar, both species having dark plumage and dark eyes. The Daurian tends to be darker, with a less contrasting nape than the Western.{{cite book |last1=Madge |first1=Steve |last2=Burn |first2=Hilary |title=Crows and Jays: A Guide to the Crows, Jays and Magpies of the World |date=1994 |publisher=[[A & C Black]] |location=London, United Kingdom |isbn=978-0-7136-3999-5 |pages=136–37}} ===Vocalisations=== [[File:Western Jackdaws on Inisheer (flying).jpg|thumb|Western jackdaw calling in flight on [[Inisheer]], Ireland]] Western jackdaws are voluble birds. The main call, frequently given in flight, is a metallic and squeaky ''chyak-chyak'' or ''kak-kak''. This is a contact or greeting call. A feeding call made by adults to call young, or males when offering food to their mates, has been transcribed as ''kiaw'' or ''kyow''. Females in return give a more drawn out version when begging for food from males, written as ''kyaay'', ''tchaayk'' or ''giaaaa''.[[#Cramp|Cramp]], p. 133. Perched birds often chatter together, and before settling for the night, large roosting flocks make a cackling noise. Western jackdaws also have a hoarse, drawn-out [[alarm call]], ''arrrrr'' or ''kaaaarr'', used when warning of predators or when mobbing them. Nestlings begin making a soft ''cheep'' at about a week of age. As they grow, their voice becomes louder until their call is a penetrating screech around day 18. After this, the voice deepens and softens.[[#Cramp|Cramp]], p. 134. From day 25, the young cease calling and become silent if they hear an unfamiliar noise. The European jackdaw can be trained to imitate human speech.{{cite journal|last1=Ogilvie|first1=Richard B.|last2=Eisenbeis|first2=Roland F.|date=6 April 1968|title=Talking Birds|url=http://www.newton.dep.anl.gov/natbltn/300-399/nb301.htm|journal=Nature Bulletin|issue=301–A|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140728230215/http://www.newton.dep.anl.gov/natbltn/300-399/nb301.htm|archive-date=28 July 2014|access-date=1 July 2014}} ==Distribution and habitat== The western jackdaw is found from [[Maghreb|Northwest Africa]] through all of Europe, except for the [[subarctic]] north, and eastwards through [[central Asia]] to the eastern [[Himalayas]] and [[Lake Baikal]]. To the east, it occurs throughout [[Turkey]], the [[Caucasus]], [[Iran]], [[Iraq]], [[Afghanistan]], [[Pakistan]], and northwestern [[India]]. However, it is regionally extinct in [[Malta]] and [[Tunisia]]. The range is vast, with an estimated global extent between {{convert|1|and(-)|10|e6km2|e6mi2|sigfig=1|abbr=off}}. It has a large global population, with an estimated 15.6 to 45 million individuals in Europe alone.{{cite web |url=http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/species/index.html?action=SpcHTMDetails.asp&sid=5758 |title=BirdLife International (2012) Species factsheet: ''Corvus monedula''|publisher=Downloaded from birdlife.org |access-date=2012-05-01}} Censuses of bird populations in marginal uplands in Great Britain show that western jackdaws greatly increased in numbers between the 1970s and 2010, although this increase may be related to recovery from previous periods when they were regarded as pests.{{Cite journal | last1 = Henderson | first1 = I. G.| last2 = Fuller | first2 = R. J. | last3 = Conway | first3 = G. J. | last4 = Gough | first4 = S. J. | title = Evidence for declines in populations of grassland-associated birds in marginal upland areas of Britain | doi = 10.1080/00063650409461327 | journal = Bird Study | volume = 51 | pages = 12–19 | year = 2004 | s2cid = 83909080| doi-access = free }} The text reports large declines among summer populations between 1968-80 and 2000. The UK population was estimated at 2.5 million individuals in 1998, up from 780,000 in 1970.{{Cite journal | last1 = Newson | first1 = Stuart E. | last2 = Woodburn | first2 = Rick J. W. | last3 = Noble | first3 = David G. | last4 = Baillie | first4 = Stephen R. | last5 = Gregory | first5 = Richard D. | title = Evaluating the Breeding Bird Survey for producing national population size and density estimates| doi = 10.1080/00063650509461373 | journal = Bird Study | volume = 52 | pages = 42–54 | year = 2005 | s2cid = 87696240 | doi-access = free }} The BBS has potential for producing better estimates of habitat-specific densities and population sizes for many UK bird populations than those available previously. [[File:Western Jackdaw on Inisheer (perched).jpg|thumb|A western jackdaw on [[Inisheer]], Ireland]] Most populations are resident, but the northern and eastern populations are more migratory, relocating to wintering areas between September and November and returning between February and early May.[[#Cramp|Cramp]], p. 124. Their range expands northwards into Russia to Siberia during summer and retracts in winter. They are vagrants to the [[Faroe Islands]], particularly in the winter and spring, and occasionally to [[Iceland]]. Elsewhere, western jackdaws congregate over winter in the [[Ural River|Ural Valley]] in northwestern Kazakhstan, the northern [[Caspian Depression|Caspian]], and the [[Tian Shan]] region of western China. They are winter visitors to the [[Quetta|Quetta Valley]] in western Pakistan, and are winter vagrants to [[Lebanon]], where they were first recorded in 1962.{{cite journal |last1=Ramadan-Jaradi |first1=Ghassan |last2=Bara |first2=Thierry |last3=Ramadan-Jaradi |first3=Mona |title=Revised Checklist of the Birds of Lebanon |journal=Sandgrouse |date=2008 |volume=30 |issue=1 |pages=22–69}} In [[Syria]], they are winter vagrants and rare residents with some confirmed breeding taking place.{{cite journal |last1=Murdoch |first1=D. A. |first2=K. F. |last2=Betton |title=A Checklist of the Birds of Syria |journal=Sandgrouse |issue=Supplement 2 |date=2008 |page=12}} The subspecies ''soemmerringii'' occurs in south-central Siberia and extreme northwestern China and is accidental to [[Hokkaido]], [[Japan]].{{cite book |last=Brazil |first=Mark |title=The Birds of East Asia |date=2009 |publisher=A&C Black |isbn=978-0-7136-7040-0 |location=London, United Kingdom |page=308}} A small number of western jackdaws reached northeastern North America in the 1980s and have been found from [[Atlantic Canada]] to [[Pennsylvania]].{{cite book |last1=Dunn |first1=John L. |last2=Alderfer |first2=Jonathan |title=National Geographic Field Guide to the Birds of North America |edition=5th |date=2006 |location=Washington, D.C. |publisher=National Geographic Society |page=326}} They have also occurred as vagrants in [[Gibraltar]], [[Mauritania]], and [[Saint Pierre and Miquelon]], and one is reported to have been seen in [[Egypt]]. Western jackdaws inhabit wooded [[steppe]]s, pastures, cultivated land, coastal cliffs, and towns. They thrive when forested areas are cleared and converted to fields and open areas. Habitats with a mix of large trees, buildings, and open ground are preferred; open fields are left to the [[rook (bird)|rook]], and more wooded areas to the [[Eurasian jay]] (''Garrulus glandarius''). Along with other corvids such as the rook, [[common raven]] (''Corvus corax''), and [[hooded crow]] (''C. cornix''), some western jackdaws spend the winter in urban parks; populations measured in three urban parks in [[Warsaw]] show increases from October to December, possibly due to western jackdaws migrating there from areas further north.{{cite journal|last1=Żmihorski |first1=Michał |last2=Halba |first2=Ryszard |last3=Mazgajski |first3=Tomasz D. |date=2010 |title=Long-term Spatio-temporal Dynamics of Corvids Wintering in Urban Parks of Warsaw, Poland |journal=Ornis Fennica |volume=87 |issue=2 |pages=61–68 |doi=10.51812/of.133743 |url=http://www.ornisfennica.org/pdf/vol87-2/3Zmihorski.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111007082943/http://www.ornisfennica.org/pdf/vol87-2/3Zmihorski.pdf |archive-date=7 October 2011 }} The same data from Warsaw, collected from 1977 to 2003, showed that the wintering western jackdaw population had increased four-fold. The cause of the increase is unknown, but a reduction in the number of rooks may have benefited the species locally, or rooks overwintering in [[Belarus]] may have caused western jackdaws to relocate to Warsaw.{{cite journal |last1=Mazgajski |first1=Tomasz D. |last2=Żmihorski |first2=Michał |last3=Halba |first3=Ryszard |last4=Wozniak |first4=Agnieszka |date=2008 |title=Long-term Population Trends of Corvids Wintering in Urban Parks in Central Poland |journal=Polish Journal of Ecology |volume=56 |issue=3 |pages=521–26 |url=http://www.miiz.waw.pl/pliki/pracownicy/zmihorski/Mazgajski_CorvidsWarsawWinteringTrends_PolJEcol2008.pdf }} === Fossil record === [[Fossil]]s of the western jackdaw are [[Knowledge|known]] from the [[Late Pleistocene]], specifically [[Marine isotope stages|MIS 3]] of the [[Sistema Ibérico|Iberian Range]], in a fossil assemblage believed to represent an accumulation of bones made by diurnal birds-of-prey.{{Cite journal |last=Núñez-Lahuerta |first=Carmen |last2=Cuenca-Bescós |first2=Gloria |last3=Sauqué |first3=Víctor |last4=Galán |first4=Julia |date=17 August 2016 |title=Avian remains from the Upper Pleistocene (MIS3) site of Aguilón P-7, south of the Ebro River, Spain |url=http://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/08912963.2015.1028928 |journal=[[Historical Biology]] |language=en |volume=28 |issue=6 |pages=774–786 |doi=10.1080/08912963.2015.1028928 |issn=0891-2963 |access-date=5 October 2025 |via=Taylor and Francis Online|url-access=subscription }} ==Behaviour== [[File:Coloeus monedula -Bushy Park, London Borough of Richmond upon Thames, England -several-8 (1).jpg|left|thumb|A family group in [[Bushy Park]], London.]] [[File:2013 Fliegende Dohle.JPG|thumb|A western jackdaw in flight.]] Generally wary of people in the forest or countryside, western jackdaws are much tamer in urban areas. Highly gregarious, western jackdaws are generally seen in flocks of varying sizes, though males and females pair-bond for life and pairs stay together within flocks.{{cite journal |last1=Henderson |first1=I. G. |last2=Hart |first2=P. J. B. |last3=Burke |first3=T. |title=Strict Monogamy in a Semi-Colonial Passerine: The Jackdaw ''Corvus monedula'' |date=2000 |journal=Journal of Avian Biology |jstor=3676991 |doi=10.1034/j.1600-048X.2000.310209.x |volume=31 |issue=2 |pages=177–182}} Flocks increase in size in autumn and birds congregate at dusk for [[communal roosting]], with up to several thousand individuals gathering at one site. At [[Uppsala]], Sweden, 40,000 birds have been recorded at a single winter roost with mated pairs often settling together for the night.[[#Cramp|Cramp]], p. 129. Western jackdaws frequently congregate with hooded crows or rooks, the latter particularly when migrating or roosting. They have been recorded foraging with the [[common starling]] (''Sturnus vulgaris''), [[Northern lapwing]] (''Vanellus vanellus''), and [[common gull]] (''Larus canus'') in northwestern England. Flocks are targets of coordinated hunting by pairs of [[lanner falcon]]s (''Falco biarmicus''), although larger groups are more able to elude the [[Predation|predators]].{{cite journal |last=Leonardi |first=Giovanni |date=1999 |title=Cooperative Hunting of Jackdaws by the Lanner Falcon (''Falco biarmicus'') |journal=Journal of Raptor Research |volume=33 |issue=2 |pages=123–27 |url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/jrr/v033n02/p00123-p00127.pdf }} Western jackdaws sometimes mob and drive off larger birds such as European magpies, common ravens, or [[Egyptian vulture]]s (''Neophron percnopterus''); one gives an alarm call which alerts its conspecifics to gather and attack as a group.[[#Cramp|Cramp]], p. 132. Occasionally, a sick or injured western jackdaw is mobbed until it is killed. In his book ''[[King Solomon's Ring (nonfiction)|King Solomon's Ring]]'', [[Konrad Lorenz]] described and analysed the complex social interactions in a western jackdaw flock that lived around his house in [[Sankt Andrä-Wördern|Altenberg]], [[Austria]]. He ringed them for identification and caged them in the winter to prevent their annual [[Bird migration|migration]]. He found that the birds have a [[Dominance hierarchy|linear hierarchical group structure]], with higher-ranked individuals dominating lower-ranked birds, and pair-bonded birds sharing the same rank.{{cite book |last=Lorenz |first=Konrad |author-link=Konrad Lorenz |translator=Marjorie Kerr Wilson |title=King Solomon's Ring: New Light on Animal Ways |chapter=11. The Perennial Retainers |orig-date=1952 |date=1964 |publisher=Methuen |location=London, United Kingdom|title-link=King Solomon's Ring (nonfiction) }} Young males establish their individual status before pairing with females. Upon pairing, the female assumes the same social position as her partner. Unmated females are the lowest members in the pecking order, and are the last to have access to food and shelter. Lorenz noted one case in which a male, absent during the dominance struggles and pair bondings, returned to the flock, became the dominant male, and chose one of two unpaired females for a mate. This female immediately assumed a dominant position in the social hierarchy and demonstrated this by pecking others. According to Lorenz, the most significant factor in social behaviour was the immediate and intuitive grasp of the new hierarchy by each of the western jackdaws in the flock. ===Social displays=== Social hierarchy in western jackdaw flocks is determined by supplanting, fighting, and [[threat display]]s—several of which have been described. In the ''bill-up posture'', the western jackdaw tilts its bill and head upwards and sleeks its plumage. Indicating both appeasement and assertiveness, the posture is used by birds intending to enter feeding flocks. A ''bill-down posture'' is another commonly used [[agonistic behaviour]]. In this display, a bird lowers its bill and erects its nape and head feathers, and sometimes slightly lifts its wings. Western jackdaws often face off in this posture until one backs down or a fight ensues. In the ''forward-threat posture'', a bird holds its body horizontally and thrusts its head forwards. In intense versions, the bird ruffles its feathers and spreads or raises its tail and wings. This extreme is seen when facing off over nests or females.[[#Cramp|Cramp]], p. 130. In the ''defensive-threat posture'', the bird lowers its head and bill, spreads its tail and ruffles its feathers. ''Supplanting'' is where one bird moves in and displaces another from a perch-site. The second bird usually retreats without resorting to a fight. Western jackdaws fight by launching themselves at each other feet-first and then wrestling with their feet intertwined and pecking at each other. Other individuals gather and call noisily.[[#Cramp|Cramp]], p. 131. Western jackdaws entreat their partners to preen them by showing their nape and ruffling their head feathers. Birds mainly preen each other's head and neck. Known as ''allopreening'', this behaviour is almost always done between birds of a mated pair. ===Breeding=== [[File:Coloeus monedula -Conwy Castle, Clwyd, Wales-8 (2).jpg|right|thumb|Occupying a hole in a wall at [[Conwy Castle]], Wales.]] [[File:Jackdaw brood.jpg|thumb|right|Nest with a chick and eggs.]] [[File:Corvus monedula MWNH 2325.JPG|thumb|Eggs, Collection [[Museum Wiesbaden]]]] [[File:Corvus monedula fledgling 2.jpg|right|thumb|Fledgling ''C. m. spermologus'' in southern England.]] Western jackdaws become sexually mature in their second year. Genetic analysis of pairs and offspring shows no evidence of [[extra-pair copulation]] and there is little evidence for couple separation even after multiple instances of reproductive failure.{{cite journal|first1=Nathan J.|last1= Emery|first2= Amanda M.|last2= Seed|first3=Auguste M.P.|last3= von Bayern|first4=Nicola S.|last4= Clayton|title=Cognitive Adaptations of Social Bonding in Birds|journal=[[Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society B]] |date=29 April 2007 |volume=362|issue=1480|pages=489–505|doi=10.1098/rstb.2006.1991|pmid= 17255008|pmc=2346513}} Some pairs do separate in the first few months, but almost all pairings of over six months' duration are lifelong, ending only when a partner dies.[[#Cramp|Cramp]], p. 128. Widowed or separated birds fare badly, often being ousted from nests or territories and unable to rear broods alone. Western jackdaws usually breed in [[Bird colony|colonies]] with pairs collaborating to find a nest site, which they then defend from other pairs and predators during most of the year. They nest in cavities in trees or cliffs, in ruined or occupied buildings and in chimneys, the common feature being a sheltered site for the nest. The availability of suitable sites influences their presence in a locale. They may also use church steeples for nesting, a fact reported in verse by 18th century English poet [[William Cowper]]: {{Blockquote| A great frequenter of the church,
Where, bishoplike, he finds a perch,
And dormitory too.{{cite book |title=The Poetical Works of William Cowper |volume=2 |location=London, United Kingdom |publisher=William Pickering |date=1853 |page=336}}}} Nest platforms can attain a great size. A mated pair usually constructs a nest by improving a crevice by dropping sticks into it; it is then built on top of the platform formed.{{cite book |last=Wilmore |first=S. Bruce |title=Crows, Jays, Ravens and their Relatives |location=London, United Kingdom |publisher=David and Charles |date=1977}} This behaviour has led to the blocking of chimneys and even resulted in nests crashing down into fireplaces, sometimes with birds still on them.{{cite book |last=Greenoak |first=F. |title=All the Birds of the Air; the Names, Lore and Literature of British Birds |location=London, United Kingdom |publisher=Book Club Associates |date=1979 |isbn=978-0-233-97037-0}} In his ''[[The Natural History and Antiquities of Selborne|The Natural History of Selborne]]'', [[Gilbert White]] notes that western jackdaws used to nest in crevices beneath the [[lintel]]s of [[Stonehenge]], and describes an example of the bird using a rabbit burrow for nesting. The species has been recorded outcompeting the [[tawny owl]] (''Strix aluco'') for nest sites in the Netherlands.{{Cite journal | last1 = Koning | first1 = F. J. | last2 = Koning | first2 = H. J. | last3 = Baeyens | first3 = G. | doi = 10.5253/078.097.0408 | title = Long-Term Study on Interactions between Tawny Owls (''Strix aluco''), Jackdaws (''Corvus monedula'') and Northern Goshawks (''Accipiter gentilis'') | journal = Ardea | volume = 97 | issue = 4 | pages = 453–456 | year = 2009| s2cid = 84634921 | doi-access = free }} They can take over old nest sites of the [[black woodpecker]] (''Dryocopus martius''){{Cite journal | last1 = Johnsson | first1 = Kristina| title = Colonial Breeding and Nest Predation in the Jackdaw ''Corvus monedula'' using old Black Woodpecker ''Dryocopus martius'' Holes | doi = 10.1111/j.1474-919X.1994.tb01100.x | journal = Ibis | volume = 136 | issue = 3 | pages = 313–17 | year = 1994 }} and [[stock dove]] (''Columba oenas''). Breeding colonies may also edge out those of the red-billed chough, but in turn be ousted by larger corvids such as the carrion crow, rook or magpie. Nests are lined with hair, wool, dead grass and many other materials.{{cite book |title=The Birds of the British Isles and their Eggs |url=https://archive.org/details/birdsofbritish00cowa |last=Coward |first=T. A. |date=1920 |publisher=Frederick Warne & Co Ltd. |location=London |pages=[https://archive.org/details/birdsofbritish00cowa/page/28 28]–30}} The [[Egg (biology)|egg]]s are a lighter colour than those of other corvids,[[#Goodwin|Goodwin]], p. 47 being smooth, a glossy pale blue or blue-green with darker speckles ranging from dark brown to olive or grey-violet.[[#Cramp|Cramp]], p. 135. Egg size and weight varies slightly between subspecies; those of subspecies ''monedula'' average {{convert|35.0|x|24.7|mm}} and {{convert|11.1|g|2|abbr=on}} in weight, those of subspecies ''soemmerringii'' {{convert|34.8|x|25.0|mm}} in size and {{convert|11.3|g|2|abbr=on}} in weight, and those of subspecies ''spermologus'' {{convert|35.0|x|25.2|mm}} in size and {{convert|11.5|g|2|abbr=on}} in weight. Clutches usually contain 4 or 5 eggs, although a Slovak study found clutch sizes ranging from 2 to 9 eggs.{{cite journal |last1=Tryjanowski |first1=Piotr |last2=Kuczynski |first2=Lechosław |last3=Antczak |first3=Marcin |last4=Skoracki |first4=Maciej |last5=Hromada |first5=Martin |date=2001 |title=Within-clutch Repeatability of Egg Dimensions in the Jackdaw ''Corvus monedula'': a Study Based on a Museum Collection |journal=Biologia |volume=56 |issue=2 |pages=211–15 |url=http://www.planetofbirds.com/jackdaw-article-2}} The eggs are incubated by the female for 17–18 days until hatching as naked [[altricial]] chicks, which are completely dependent on the adults for food. They [[fledge]] after 28–35 days, and the parents continue to feed them for another four weeks or so. Western jackdaws hatch asynchronously and incubation begins before clutch completion, which often leads to the death of the last-hatched young. If the supply of food is low, parental investment in the brood is kept to a minimum as little energy is wasted on feeding a chick that is unlikely to survive.{{cite journal |last=Gibbons |first=David Wingfield |title=Hatching Asynchrony Reduces Parental Investment in the Jackdaw |journal=Journal of Animal Ecology |date=June 1987 |volume=53 |issue=2 |pages=403–14 |jstor=5056 |doi=10.2307/5056}} Replacement clutches are very rarely laid in the event of clutch failure. The [[great spotted cuckoo]] (''Clamator glandarius'') has been recorded as a [[brood parasite]] of the western jackdaw, depositing its eggs in their nests in Spain and Israel.{{cite book |last=Arias-de-Reyna |first=Luis |title=Parasitic Birds and their Hosts: Studies in Coevolution |editor1-last=Rothstein |editor1-first=Stephen I. |editor2-last=Robinson |editor2-first=Scott Kuehner |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=Oxford, United Kingdom |date=1998 |pages=129–42 |chapter=Coevolution of the Great Spotted Cuckoo and its Hosts |isbn=978-0-19-509976-8}}{{Cite journal | last1 = Charter | first1 = M. | last2 = Bouskila | first2 = A. | last3 = Aviel | first3 = S. | last4 = Leshem | first4 = Y. | title = First Record of Eurasian Jackdaw (''Corvus monedula'') Parasitism by the Great Spotted Cuckoo (''Clamator glandarius'') in Israel | doi = 10.1676/04-065 | jstor=20060085| journal = The Wilson Bulletin | volume = 117 | issue = 2 | pages = 201–204 | year = 2005 | s2cid = 85885434 }} Nest robbers include the common raven in Spain, tawny owl, and [[least weasel]] (''Mustela nivalis'') in England, and [[brown rat]] (''Rattus norvegicus'') in Finland.[[#Cramp|Cramp]], p. 136. The [[European pine marten]] (''Martes martes'') raids isolated nests in Sweden but is less successful when nests are part of a colony. ===Feeding=== [[File:Corvus monedula 009.jpg|thumb|''C. m. soemmerringii'', foraging in pasture in Russia.]] Foraging takes place mostly on the ground in open areas and to some extent in trees. [[Landfill]] sites, bins, streets, and gardens are also visited, more often early in the morning when there are fewer people about. Various feeding methods are employed, such as jumping, pecking, clod-turning and scattering, probing the soil, and occasionally, digging. [[Fly|Flies]] around [[Cow dung|cow pats]] are caught by jumping from the ground or at times by dropping vertically from a few metres onto the cow pat. [[Earthworm]]s are not usually extracted from the ground by western jackdaws but are eaten from freshly ploughed soil. Jackdaws will ride on the backs of sheep and other mammals, seeking [[tick]]s as well as actively gathering wool or hair for nests, and will catch flying ants in flight. Compared with other corvids, the western jackdaw spends more time exploring and turning over objects with its bill; it also has a straighter and less downturned bill and increased binocular vision which are advantageous for this foraging strategy.{{cite journal |last1=Kulemeyer |first1=Christoph |last2=Asbahr |first2=Kolja |last3=Gunz |first3=Philipp |last4=Frahnert |first4=Sylke |last5=Bairlein |first5=Franz |date=2009 |title=Functional Morphology and Integration of Corvid Skulls – a 3D Geometric Morphometric Approach |journal=Frontiers in Zoology |volume=6 |issue=2 |doi=10.1186/1742-9994-6-2 |pmid=19128499 |page=2|pmc=2651141 |doi-access=free }} The western jackdaw tends to feed on small [[invertebrate]]s up to {{convert|18|mm}} in length that are found above ground, including various species of [[Coleoptera|beetle]] (particularly cockchafers of the genus ''[[Melolontha]]'',[[#Cramp|Cramp]], p. 125. and [[Curculionidae|weevil]] [[larva]]e and [[pupa]]e.{{cite journal |last=Lockie |first=J. D. |title=The Food and Feeding Behaviour of the Jackdaw, Rook and Carrion Crow |journal=The Journal of Animal Ecology |volume=25 |issue=2 |date=November 1956 |pages=421–28 |doi=10.2307/1935 |jstor=1935}}), ''[[Diptera]]'', and ''[[Lepidoptera]]'' species, as well as snails and spiders. Also eaten are small rodents, [[bat]]s,{{cite journal|author=Mikula P.|year=2013|trans-title=Western Jackdaw (''Corvus monedula'') attacking bats (Chiroptera): observations from Bardejov, northeastern Slovakia|title=Pozorované útoky kavky tmavej (''Corvus monedula'') na netopiere (Chiroptera) v Bardejove, severovýchodné Slovensko|journal=Sylvia|volume=49|pages=157–159|url=http://www.cso.cz/wpimages/other/sylvia49_7Mikula.pdf|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160309064753/http://www.cso.cz/wpimages/other/sylvia49_7Mikula.pdf|archive-date=2016-03-09}} the eggs and chicks of birds, and [[carrion]] such as [[roadkill]]. Vegetable items consumed include farm grains (barley, wheat and oats), weed seeds, elderberries, acorns, and various cultivated fruits. Examination of the [[gizzard]]s of western jackdaws shot in [[Cyprus]] in spring and summer revealed a diet of cereals (predominantly wheat) and insects (notably [[cicada]]s and [[beetle]]s).{{cite journal |last=Hadjisterkotis |first=E. |date=2003 |title=The Effect of Corvid Shooting on the Populations of Owls, Kestrels and Cuckoos in Cyprus, with Notes on Corvid Diet |journal=Zeitschrift für Jagdwissenschaft |volume=49 |issue=1 |pages=50–60 |doi=10.1007/BF02192013|s2cid=42994894 }} The diet averages 84% plant material except when breeding, when the main food source is insects. A study in southern Spain examining western jackdaw pellets found that they contained significant amounts of [[Silicon dioxide|silicaceous]] and [[Calcium carbonate|calcareous]] grit to aid digestion of vegetable food and supply dietary calcium.{{cite journal |last1=Soler |first1=Juan José |last2=Soler |first2=Manuel |last3=Martínez |first3=Juan Gabriel |date=1993 |title=Grit Ingestion and Cereal Consumption in Five Corvid Species |journal=Ardea |volume=81 |issue=2 |pages=143–49 |url=http://www.eeza.csic.es/eeza/documentos/soler_4752.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111003035023/http://www.eeza.csic.es/eeza/documentos/soler_4752.pdf |archive-date=2011-10-03 }} Opportunistic and highly adaptable, the western jackdaw varies its diet markedly depending on available food sources.[[#Cramp|Cramp]], p. 127. They have been recorded taking eggs and nestlings from the nests of the [[skylark]] (''Alauda arvensis''),{{cite journal |last1=Praus |first1=Libor |last2=Weidinger |first2=Karel |date=2010 |title=Predators and Nest Success of Sky Larks ''Alauda arvensis'' in Large Arable Fields in the Czech Republic |journal=Bird Study |doi=10.1080/00063657.2010.506208 |volume=57 |issue=4 |pages=525–30 |s2cid=84660427 |url=http://www.zoologie.upol.cz/clanky_pdf/Weidinger_Bird_Study_2010.pdf |access-date=2011-07-04 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111005191208/http://www.zoologie.upol.cz/clanky_pdf/Weidinger_Bird_Study_2010.pdf |archive-date=2011-10-05 }} [[Manx shearwater]] (''Puffinus puffinus''), [[razorbill]] (''Alca torda''), [[common guillemot]] (''Uria aalge''), [[grey heron]] (''Ardea cinerea''), [[rock dove]] (''Columba livia''),{{cite journal |last1=Hetmański |first1=Tomasz |last2=Barkowska |first2=Miłosława |title=Density and Age of Breeding Pairs Influence Feral Pigeon, ''Columba livia'' Reproduction |date=2007 |journal=Folia Zoologica |volume=56 |issue=1 |pages=71–83 |url=http://www.ivb.cz/folia/56/1/71-83_MS1182.pdf |access-date=2011-08-03 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111007032930/http://www.ivb.cz/folia/56/1/71-83_MS1182.pdf |archive-date=2011-10-07 }} and [[Eurasian collared dove]] (''Streptopelia decaocto''). A field study of a large city dump on the outskirts of [[León, Spain|León]] in northwestern Spain showed that western jackdaws forage there in the early morning and at dusk, and engage in some degree of [[kleptoparasitism]].{{cite journal |last1=Baglione |first1=Vittorio |last2=Canestrari |first2=Daniela |date=2009 |title=Kleptoparasitism and Temporal Segregation of Sympatric Corvids Foraging in a Refuse Dump |journal=The Auk |volume=126 |issue=3 |pages=566–78 |doi=10.1525/auk.2009.08146|s2cid=86160426 |doi-access=free }} The [[saker falcon]] (''Falco cherrug'') has been reported stealing food from western jackdaws on powerlines in [[Vojvodina]] in Serbia.{{cite journal |last=Puzović |first=S. |date=2008 |title=Nest Occupation and Prey Grabbing by Saker Falcon (''Falco cherrug'') on Power Lines in the Province of Vojvodina (Serbia) |journal=Archives of Biological Sciences |doi= 10.2298/abs0802271p|volume=60 |issue=2 |pages=271–77 |url=http://www.doiserbia.nb.rs/img/doi/0354-4664/2008/0354-46640802271P.pdf |doi-access=free }} Western jackdaws practice active food sharing – where the initiative for the transfer lies with the donor – with a number of individuals, regardless of sex or kinship. They also share more of a preferred food than a less preferred food.{{cite journal |last1=de Kort |first1=Selvino R. |last2=Emery |first2=Nathan J. |last3=Clayton |first3=Nicola S. |title=Food Sharing in Jackdaws, ''Corvus monedula'': What, Why and with Whom? |date=2006 |journal=Animal Behaviour |volume=72 |issue=2 |pages=297–304 |doi=10.1016/j.anbehav.2005.10.016|s2cid=53160564 }} The active giving of food by most birds is found mainly in the context of parental care and courtship. Western jackdaws show much higher levels of active giving than has been documented for other species, including [[Common chimpanzee|chimpanzee]]s. The function of this behaviour is not fully understood, though it has been found to be detached from nutrition and compatible with hypotheses of [[Mutualism (biology)|mutualism]], reciprocity and harassment avoidance. It has also been proposed that food sharing may be motivated by prestige enhancement.{{cite conference |last1=von Bayern |first1=A. M. P. |last2=de Kort |first2=S. R. |last3=Clayton |first3=N. S. |last4=Emery |first4=N. J. |title=Frequent food- and Object-sharing during Jackdaw (''Corvus monedula'') Socialisation |url=http://www.behav.org/IEC/default.php?proc=search&search=a_num&id=406 |conference=Ethological Conference, Budapest, Hungary, 2005 |conference-url=http://www.behav.org/IEC/default.php}} ==Parasites and diseases== Western jackdaws have learned to peck open the foil caps of milk bottles left on the doorsteps after delivery by the milkman. The bacterium ''[[Campylobacter jejuni]]'' has been isolated from their beaks and [[cloaca]]e so milk can become contaminated as they drink. This activity was linked to cases of ''Campylobacter'' [[gastroenteritis]] in [[Gateshead]] in northeast England{{Cite journal | last1 = Hudson | first1 = S. J. | last2 = Sobo | first2 = A. O. | last3 = Russel | first3 = K. | last4 = Lightfoot | first4 = N. F. | title = Jackdaws as potential source of milk-borne ''Campylobacter jejuni'' infection | doi = 10.1016/0140-6736(90)91162-4 | journal = The Lancet | volume = 335 | issue = 8698 | page = 1160 | year = 1990 | pmid = 1971891 | s2cid = 41216961 }} and led the [[Department of Health (United Kingdom)|Department of Health]] to suggest that milk from bottles which had been pecked open should be discarded. It was recommended that steps be taken to prevent birds from pecking bottles open in the future.{{Cite journal | doi = 10.1016/0140-6736(91)92541-9 | title = The thieving magpie (and jackdaw) | journal = The Lancet | volume = 337 | issue = 8745 | page = 844 | year = 1991 | s2cid = 54248330 }} An outbreak of a gastrointestinal illness in Spain which was causing mortalities in humans has been linked to western jackdaws. During a [[Autopsy|post-mortem]] on an affected bird, a [[Polyomaviridae|polyomavirus]] was isolated from the [[spleen]]. The illness appeared to be a co-infection of this with ''[[Salmonella]]'' and the virus has been provisionally named the [[crow polyomavirus]] (CPyV).{{Cite journal | last1 = Johne | first1 = R. | last2 = Wittig | first2 = W. | last3 = Fernandez-De-Luco | first3 = D. | last4 = Hofle | first4 = U. | last5 = Muller | first5 = H. | title = Characterization of Two Novel Polyomaviruses of Birds by Using Multiply Primed Rolling-Circle Amplification of Their Genomes | doi = 10.1128/JVI.80.7.3523-3531.2006 | journal = Journal of Virology | volume = 80 | issue = 7 | pages = 3523–3531 | year = 2006 | pmid = 16537620| pmc =1440385 }} [[Segmented filamentous bacteria]] have been isolated from the small intestine of a western jackdaw, although their [[pathogen]]icity or role is unknown.{{Cite journal |last1 = Klaasen |first1 = H. L. |last2 = Koopman |first2 = J. P. |last3 = Van Den Brink |first3 = M. E. |last4 = Bakker |first4 = M. H. |last5 = Poelma |first5 = F. G. |last6 = Beynen |first6 = A. C. |title = Intestinal, segmented, filamentous bacteria in a wide range of vertebrate species |journal = Laboratory Animals |volume = 27 |issue = 2 |pages = 141–50 |year = 1993 |pmid = 8501895 |doi = 10.1258/002367793780810441 |s2cid = 39632270 |doi-access= free }} ==Pest control== The western jackdaw has been hunted as a pest, though not as heavily culled as other species of corvid.{{cite book |last1=Marzluff |first1=John M. |last2=Angell |first2=Tony |title=In the Company of Crows and Ravens |publisher=Yale University Press |location=New Haven, Connecticut & London, United Kingdom |year=2005 |isbn=978-0-300-10076-1 |chapter=Cultural Connections |chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FrG8pIQ5WJkC&pg=PT234}} After a series of poor harvests in the early 1500s, [[Henry VIII of England|{{nowrap|Henry VIII}}]] introduced a Vermin Act in 1532 "ordeyned to dystroye Choughes (i.e. jackdaws), Crowes and Rokes" to protect grain crops from their predations. Western jackdaws were notorious for their fondness for fruit, especially cherries. This act was adopted piecemeal, but [[Elizabeth I of England|{{nowrap|Elizabeth I}}]] passed the ''Act for the Preservation of Grayne'' in 1566 which was enforced more vigorously. The species was hunted for threatening grain crops and for propensity for nesting in belfries until the mid-20th century. Particularly large numbers were culled in the county of [[Norfolk]]. Western jackdaws were also culled on game estates for raiding the nests of other birds for eggs.{{cite book|last= Lovegrove|first=Roger|title=Silent Fields: The Long Decline of a Nation's Wildlife|publisher=Oxford University Press|location=Oxford, United Kingdom|year=2007|pages=79–81, 162–63|isbn=978-0-19-852071-9|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qQCV6eBgD9YC&pg=PA80}} In a 2003 dissertation on public opinion of corvids, Antonia Hereth notes that the German naturalist [[Alfred Brehm]] considered the western jackdaw to be a lovable bird, and did not attribute any negative impacts of to this species with regard to agriculture.{{cite book |last=Hereth |first=Antonia |title=Das Bild der Rabenvögel (Corvidae) in der heutigen Gesellschaft |language=de |year=2003 |publisher=Justus – Liebig – Universität Gießen |pages=10, 25 |url=http://geb.uni-giessen.de/geb/volltexte/2003/1143/index.html}} The western jackdaw is one of a very small number of birds that can legally be used as decoys or trapped in cages in the United Kingdom. The other pest species that can be controlled by trapping are the [[carrion crow|crow]], [[Eurasian jay|jay]], [[Eurasian magpie|magpie]] and [[Rook (bird)|rook]]. An authorised person does not need to prove that the birds were a nuisance before trapping them, but must comply with the provisions of the [[Wildlife and Countryside Act 1981]].{{cite web |url=http://www.basc.org.uk/en/codes-of-practice/trapping-pest-birds.cfm |title=Trapping Pest Birds |publisher=BASC: The British Association for Shooting and Conservation |access-date=19 April 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110810150726/http://www.basc.org.uk/en/codes-of-practice/trapping-pest-birds.cfm |archive-date=10 August 2011 }} As of 2003, the western jackdaw was listed as a potential species for targeted hunting in the European Union [[Birds Directive]], and hunting has been encouraged by German hunting associations. Permission to shoot western jackdaws in spring and summer exists in Cyprus, despite them not preying on gamebirds as is commonly believed. ==Cultural depictions and folklore== [[File:Borrowed plumes.jpg|thumb|left|[[Harrison Weir]]'s 1881 illustration of a vain jackdaw wearing peacock feathers for "[[The Bird in Borrowed Feathers]]" (also known as "The Vain Jackdaw") fable{{cite web |title=Aesop's Fables: The Vain Jackdaw |publisher=[[BBC School Radio]] |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/learning/schoolradio/subjects/english/aesops_fables/9-16/vain_jackdaw |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130506082324/http://www.bbc.co.uk/learning/schoolradio/subjects/english/aesops_fables/9-16/vain_jackdaw |archive-date=6 May 2013 |access-date=5 June 2012}}]] [[File:Sauvo.vaakuna.svg|thumb|upright|A jackdaw pictured in the [[coat of arms]] of the [[Sauvo|Sauvo municipality]]]] An ancient Greek and Roman adage runs "The swans will sing when the jackdaws are silent", meaning that wise or educated people will only speak once the foolish have become quiet.{{cite book |title=Collected Works of Erasmus: Adages: Ivi1 to Ix100 |translator=Roger A. Mynors |publisher=University of Toronto Press |date=1989 |page=314|title-link=Erasmus }} In [[Ancient Greece|Ancient Greek]] [[folklore]], a jackdaw can be caught with a dish of oil. A [[Narcissus (mythology)|narcissistic]] creature, it falls in while looking at its own reflection.{{cite book |last=Thompson |first=D'Arcy Wentworth |title=A Glossary of Greek Birds |url=https://archive.org/details/glossaryofgreekb00thom |location=Oxford, United Kingdom |date=1895|page=[https://archive.org/details/glossaryofgreekb00thom/page/89 89]}} The mythical [[Arne Sithonis|Princess Arne Sithonis]] was bribed with gold by [[Minos|King Minos]] of [[Crete]], and was punished by the gods for her greed by being transformed into an equally avaricious jackdaw, who still seeks shiny things.{{cite book |last=Graves |first=Robert |author-link=Robert Graves |title=Greek Myths |date=1955 |publisher=Penguin |location=London, United Kingdom |isbn=978-0-14-001026-8 |page=[https://archive.org/details/greekmythsvolume00robe/page/308 308] |chapter=Scylla and Nisus |chapter-url=https://archive.org/details/greekmythsvolume00robe/page/308 }} The Roman poet [[Ovid]] described jackdaws as harbingers of rain in his poetic work ''[[Amores (Ovid)|Amores]]''.{{cite book |last=de Vries |first=Ad |title=Dictionary of Symbols and Imagery |date=1976 |page=[https://archive.org/details/dictionaryofsymb0000vrie/page/275 275] |publisher=North-Holland Publishing Company |location=Amsterdam, Netherlands |isbn=978-0-7204-8021-4 |url=https://archive.org/details/dictionaryofsymb0000vrie/page/275 }} [[Pliny the Elder|Pliny]] notes how the [[Thessalian]]s, [[Illyrians]], and [[Lemnian]]s cherished jackdaws for destroying grasshoppers' eggs. The [[Adriatic Veneti|Veneti]] are fabled to have bribed the jackdaws to spare their crops. In some cultures, the presence of a jackdaw on a roof is said to predict a new arrival. Alternatively, if a jackdaw settles on the roof of a house or flies down a chimney, it is considered an omen of death. Encountering a jackdaw is also an ill omen. A jackdaw standing on the vanes of a cathedral tower is said to foretell rain. The 12th-century historian [[William of Malmesbury]] records the story of a woman who, upon hearing a jackdaw chattering "more loudly than usual," grew pale and became fearful of suffering a "dreadful calamity", and that "while yet speaking, the messenger of her misfortunes arrived".{{cite encyclopaedia |encyclopedia=A Dictionary of Superstitions |title=Jackdaw |editor1-last=Opie |editor1-first=Iona |editor1-link=Peter and Iona Opie |editor2-last=Tatem |editor2-first=Moira |date=1996 |publisher=Oxford University Press and [[Fatih University]] |location=Oxford, United Kingdom |access-date=19 December 2008 |url=http://www.oxfordreference.com/views/ENTRY.html?subview=Main&entry=t72.e807 |url-access=subscription |isbn=978-0-19-282916-0 }} [[Czechs|Czech]] superstition formerly held that if jackdaws are seen quarreling, war will follow, and that jackdaws will not build nests at [[Sázava (Benešov District)|Sázava]] after being banished by [[Procopius of Sázava|Saint Procopius]]. The jackdaw was considered sacred in [[Welsh folklore]] as it nested in church steeples – it was shunned by the Devil because of its choice of residence.{{cite book |last=Owen |first=Elias |title=Welsh Folk-lore: a Collection of the Folk-tales and Legends of North Wales |publisher=Kessinger Publishing |date=1896 |page=324 |isbn=978-0-88305-487-1 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SiC8opYctHQC&pg=PA324}} Nineteenth century belief in [[the Fens]] held that seeing a jackdaw on the way to a wedding was a good omen for a bride.{{cite book |last=Porter |first=Enid |title=Cambridgeshire Customs and Folklore: with Fenland Material Provided |publisher=Taylor & Francis |date=1969 |pages=38–39 |isbn=978-0-7100-6201-7 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=rU4VAAAAIAAJ&pg=PA38}} The jackdaw is featured on the [[Ukraine|Ukrainian]] town of [[Halych]]'s ancient coat of arms, the town's name allegedly being derived from the [[East Slavic languages|East Slavic]] word for the bird.{{cite book|first=Paul Robert|last= Magocsi|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qgTrJm85sDQC&pg=PR15|title=Galicia: a Historical Survey and Bibliographic Guide |location=Toronto, Ontario|publisher=University of Toronto Press|year=1983|page=xv|isbn=978-0-8020-2482-4}} In ''[[The Book of Laughter and Forgetting]]'' (1979), [[Milan Kundera]] notes that [[Franz Kafka]]'s father Hermann had a sign in front of his shop with a jackdaw painted next to his name, since "kavka" means ''jackdaw'' in [[Czech language|Czech]].{{cite book|last=Gray|first=Richard T.|title=Franz Kafka Encyclopedia|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|location=Westport, Connecticut|year=2005|page=12|isbn=978-0-313-30375-3|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4ZLW5gXc0X4C&pg=PA12}} Alex K Halych 2.svg|Coat of arms of the [[Principality of Halych]] Recueil_d'armoiries_polonaises_-_COA_of_Halych_Land_crop2.png|Coat of arms of [[Halych Land (ziemia)|Halych Land]] Alex_K_Grundwald_flags_1410-07.svg|Flag of Halych land at the [[Battle of Grunwald]] zh.extract:
寒鸦（学名：Coloeus monedula）为鸦科寒鸦属的鸟类，旧时归类于鸦属寒鸦亚属，别名慈鸟、小山老鸹。全球活动范围约为15,600,000平方千米。该物种的保护状况被评为无危。


特征
寒鸦的平均体重约为233.5克，体长可达35厘米；上体除颈后羽毛呈灰白色外，其余为黑色；胸和腹部为灰白色。


分布
分布于欧洲，西亚和北非各地。栖息地包括温带草原、牧草地、城市、亚热带或热带的旱林、耕地、亚热带或热带的（低地）干草原、海崖和多岩石的离岸岛屿和温带森林。一般栖息于戈壁荒漠有绿洲的树林中、亦见于河流两岸的峻峭岸壁上以及巢主要在石质或土质的洞穴或缝隙中；冬季常和秃鼻乌鸦混合成群。该物种的模式产地在瑞典。


亚种
寒鸦包括四个认定的亚种分布于欧洲的亚种混合交配：

西疆寒鸦 C. m. soemmerringii (Fischer, 1811)。分布于中欧、东欧、西亚、南亚、蒙古以及中国大陆的新疆等地。该物种的模式产地在莫斯科。
北欧寒鸦 C. m. monedula (Linnaeus, 1758)，指名亚种，在挪威东南，瑞典南部和丹麦东北繁殖，偶尔在英国和法国过冬。
西欧寒鸦 C. m. spermologus (Vieillot, 1817)，分布于西欧和中欧，在加那利群岛和科西嘉过冬。颜色较深，在灰色领下没有白边。
北非寒鸦 C. m. cirtensis (Rothschild and Hartert, 1912)，分布于北非的摩洛哥和阿尔及利亚。
